coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent sensor for highly sensitive detection of ascorbic acid in food based on inhibiting phosphatase-like activity of Zr-based MOF
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Zhen
Wang, Jinlong
Cheng, Xing
Tang, Yue
Xia, Lian
Niu, Xiaojuan
Wu, Yuangen
coredata.dc:description: Nanozyme-based sensors for detecting ascorbic acid (AA) generally depend on the reducibility of the analyte. However, these sensors are susceptible to interference from reducing substances in food. Herein, a novel fluorescent sensor for AA detection was developed based on inhibiting the phosphatase-like activity of a Zr-based metal-organic framework (Zr-CAU-28). Hydroxyl-rich AA molecules adsorb on the surface of Zr-CAU-28 through hydrogen bonding with [Zr6O4(OH)4] cluster, leading to a decrease in the relative content of terminal hydroxyl groups within the catalytic sites. The constructed sensor exhibits a wide detection range (0.08–11 μg·mL−1) and low detection limit of 0.025 μg·mL−1. Potential interfering studies demonstrated the good selectivity of the sensor. Moreover, the fluorescent sensor can effectively detect AA in juices and vitamin C tablets, with the recovery rate ranging from 96.25 % to 108.50 %. This work represents the first application of phosphatase mimics for AA detection, offering a new strategy for food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food analysis
Zr-MOF
Phosphatase mimics
Inhibition
Fluorescent detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Inhibitory mechanism of AA on Zr-CAU-28
Inhibition of AA derivatives on Zr-CAU-28
Effect of AA on catalytic kinetics
Effect of reaction temperature and time

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) SEM image of Zr-CAU-28. (B) SEM image of Zr-CAU-28 and the corresponding EDS elemental mapping images for Zr, O, C. (C) PXRD pattern of Zr-CAU-28. (D) FT-IR spectra of Zr-CAU-28. (E) XPS spectra of Zr-CAU-28. (F) High-resolution Zr 3d spectra of the Zr-CAU-28.

(A) Fluorescence images and emission spectra of different solutions. Sample 1: Zr-CAU-28; Sample 2: 4-MUP; Sample 3: Zr-CAU-28 + 4-MUP. (B) Principal diagram of the fluorescent sensor for AA detection. (C) Fluorescence images and emission spectra of different solutions. Sample 1: Zr-CAU-28 + 4-MUP; Sample 2: Sample 1 + 1 μg·mL−1 AA; Sample 3: Sample 1 + 5 μg·mL−1 AA; Sample 4: Sample 1 + 10 μg·mL−1 AA; Sample 5: Zr-CAU-28; Sample 6: 4-MUP; Sample 7: AA; Sample 8: AA +4-MUP.

(A) Zeta potential of Zr-CAU-28 before and after AA addition. (B) FT-IR spectra of Zr-CAU-28 before and after AA addition. (C) Inhibition of AA derivatives on Zr-CAU-28 catalysis. (D) Chemical structures of AA and its derivatives used in this study. (E) XPS spectra of Zr-CAU-28 before and after AA addition. (F) Relative contents of fitting peaks in O 1 s region of Zr-CAU-28 before and after AA addition.

(A) Catalytic velocities versus the concentration of Zr-CAU-28 in the presence of various amounts of AA. (B) Michaelis-Menten curves for phosphatase-like activity of Zr-CAU-28 in the presence of various amounts of AA. (C) Lineweaver-Burk plots for phosphatase-like activity of Zr-CAU-28 in the presence of various amounts of AA. (D) Vmax and Km values of catalytic reactions in the presence of various amounts of AA.

(A) Fluorescence images of sensing solutions with different amounts of AA. (B) Fluorescence spectra of sensing systems at 380–600 nm with varying quantities of AA. (C) Relationship between the change in fluorescence intensity (F0 F) at 448 nm and AA content. Inset: Changes in fluorescence intensity at low AA concentrations (Linear equation: y = 110.27 + 63.47 x, R2 = 0.983).

(A) Fluorescence images of sensing solutions in respond to AA and different interfering substances. (B) Sensing signals of the fluorescent sensor in response to AA and different interfering substances. The content of interfering substances and AA were 10 μg·mL−1.
Determination of AA in spiked actual samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: according to the package descriptions, the content of AA in orange juice is 9.6 mg·L−1, in grapefruit juice is 224.7 mg·L−1, and in vitamin C tablets is 0.13 mg·kg−1. The units of AA content in liquid and solid samples are mg·L−1 and mg·kg−1, respectively.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent sensor for highly sensitive detection of ascorbic acid in food based on inhibiting phosphatase-like activity of Zr-based MOF

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Plant Resource Conservation and Germplasm Innovation in Mountainous Region (Ministry of Education), College of Life Sciences/Institute of Agro-bioengineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Plant Resource Conservation and Germplasm Innovation in Mountainous Region (Ministry of Education), College of Life Sciences/Institute of Agro-bioengineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Plant Resource Conservation and Germplasm Innovation in Mountainous Region (Ministry of Education), College of Life Sciences/Institute of Agro-bioengineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guizhou Xijiu Co. Ltd., Xishui 564622, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guizhou Xijiu Co. Ltd., Xishui 564622, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Liquor and Food Engineering, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Liquor and Food Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozyme-based sensors for detecting ascorbic acid (AA) generally depend on the reducibility of the analyte. However, these sensors are susceptible to interference from reducing substances in food. Herein, a novel fluorescent sensor for AA detection was developed based on inhibiting the phosphatase-like activity of a Zr-based metal-organic framework (Zr-CAU-28). Hydroxyl-rich AA molecules adsorb on the surface of Zr-CAU-28 through hydrogen bonding with [Zr6O4(OH)4] cluster, leading to a decrease in the relative content of terminal hydroxyl groups within the catalytic sites. The constructed sensor exhibits a wide detection range (0.08–11 μg·mL−1) and low detection limit of 0.025 μg·mL−1. Potential interfering studies demonstrated the good selectivity of the sensor. Moreover, the fluorescent sensor can effectively detect AA in juices and vitamin C tablets, with the recovery rate ranging from 96.25 % to 108.50 %. This work represents the first application of phosphatase mimics for AA detection, offering a new strategy for food analysis.

Ascorbic acid (AA) can inhibit the phosphatase-like activity of Zr-based metal-organic framework (Zr-CAU-28), preventing the substrate 4-MUP from being hydrolyzed to fluorescent 4-MU, which enable to establish a fluorescent sensor for AA detection in food samples.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ascorbic acid (AA), commonly known as vitamin C, is a water-soluble vitamin crucial for the life activities of plants and animals. In humans, AA serves as an effective antioxidant that can protect cells from damage induced by excessive free radicals. Moreover, AA also participates in diverse biological processes, such as iron absorption, wound healing and collagen synthesis (Kim & Lee, 2004; Sami et al., 2024). Humans primarily obtain AA through the intake of fresh fruits and vegetables, among which citrus fruits, tomatoes, and peppers are ideal sources. Additionally, AA is used as an additive to prevent food from oxidation and discoloration during storage. Incorporating an appropriate amount of AA can improve the nutritional quality of food and extend its shelf life. The World Health Organization recommends a daily intake of 45 mg or a weekly intake of 300 mg of AA (J. R. Yang et al., 2023). AA deficiency can lead to physical dysfunction and trigger a range of diseases, such as scurvy, rheumatoid arthritis, and cardiovascular disease, while excessive intake may cause diarrhea, stomach irritation, and kidney stones (Dhara & Debiprosad, 2019; Xu et al., 2021). Therefore, establishing a rapid and sensitive detection method for quantifying AA content in food is of great significance.

Traditional instrumental methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Novakova et al., 2008), liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Frenich et al., 2005), and capillary electrophoresis (Tang & Wu, 2005), can accurately quantify AA in food samples. However, these approaches commonly require costly instruments, complex sample pretreatment, and professional operators. To address these limitations, a series of sensors for AA detection have been developed based on electrochemical (Cao et al., 2024), colorimetric (Chen et al., 2022), and fluorescent techniques (Y.-L. Tian et al., 2022). Among them, fluorescence method stands out for its high sensitivity, swift response, and multi-channel detection capability. Numerous strategies employ metal ions (e.g. Fe3+, Cr6+) to quench quantum dots and utilize the redox reaction between AA and metal ions to restore the quenched fluorescence (Fong et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2020). However, their detection results are susceptible to interference from reducing substances in food. Additionally, some studies entail complex fluorescent probes, significantly raising costs and complicating preparation. Hence, it is necessary to develop a simple, accurate and sensitive fluorescent sensor for AA detection.

Nanomaterials with enzyme-like activity, termed nanozymes, have been widely applied in food analysis due to their advantages of strong catalytic activity, excellent robustness, and lower cost (Huang et al., 2019). So far, researchers have developed numerous approaches for detecting AA by utilizing the inherent oxidoreductase activity of nanozymes (Lu et al., 2020; R. Wu et al., 2022). Nanomaterials with peroxidase or oxidase activity can catalyze the oxidation of colorless 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzdine (TMB) to blue-colored ox-TMB in the presence of H2O2 or O2. AA is capable of reducing ox-TMB, causing a color change that allows for direct colorimetric detection. It should be noted that these methods still face challenges due to the potential presence of reducing substances in food. In this case, employing the diverse catalytic activities of nanozymes for detecting AA offers a viable solution. Recently, several Ce- and Zr-based nanomaterials including their oxides and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), have been reported to exhibit phosphatase-like activity (Gao et al., 2022; X. Hu, Huang, et al., 2020; Xue Tian et al., 2020; M. Xu et al., 2019). Owing to their potent ability to dephosphorylate substrates, phosphatase mimics have been applied in various research areas, such as organophosphorus compounds degradation (Trenque et al., 2019), ATP dephosphorylation (Yang et al., 2022), and pesticide detection (Gai et al., 2023; Zhu et al., 2025). If the substrate is p-nitrophenyl phosphate (p-NPP), 4-methylumbelliferyl phosphate (4-MUP), or CDP star (a chemiluminescence substrate of phosphatase), phosphatase activity can be evaluated through multiple signal modes and further applied in sensing (Y. Wu, Chen, et al., 2023). Unfortunately, the analytes utilizing the phosphatase activity of nanozymes are quite limited, and there is currently no report on the use of phosphatase-mimicking nanomaterial for AA detection. Moreover, the scarcity of phosphatase mimics limits their research in sensing application. Hence, the pursuit of novel phosphatase mimics and their application in food analysis holds significant importance.

Herein, Zr-CAU-28 (a Zr-based MOF) was synthesized via a one-pot hydrothermal method and was discovered to exhibit remarkable phosphatase-like activity. Zr-CAU-28 is able to catalyze the dephosphorylation of 4-MUP to generate fluorescent 4-methylumbelliferon (4-MU) with a maximum emission at 448 nm. Further studies revealed that AA can inhibit the phosphatase-like activity of Zr-CAU-28, causing a significant decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the catalytic system. It is speculated that hydroxyl-rich AA can adsorb on the surface of Zr-CAU-28 through hydrogen bonding, thereby reducing the number of effective catalytic sites on Zr-CAU-28. The correlation between the variation of fluorescence intensity at 448 nm and AA concentration allows for the quantitative detection of AA. In this work, the phosphatase-like activity of Zr-CAU-28 was first studied and applied to food analysis. The proposed strategy utilizes AA to inhibit the catalytic activity of Zr-CAU-28 for constructing a fluorescent sensor, rather than depending on the reducibility of AA. This strategy effectively avoids the impact of reducing substances widely distributed in food matrices, and extends the application of phosphatase-mimics in sensing fields. The constructed sensor features convenience, a low detection limit, and high specificity, thereby offering a novel approach for detecting AA in food.

ZrOCl2·8H2O, 2,5-furandicarboxylic acid (H2FDC), acetic acid, 4-MUP, p-NPP, 4-MU, anhydrous ethanol, AA, methionine (Met), glycine (Gly), lysine (Lys), aspartate (Asp), serine (Ser), leucine (Leu), proline (Pro), cysteine (Cys), glutathione (GSH), glucose (Glu), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), N-2-hydroxyethyl piperazine-N′-2-ethanesulfonic acid (HEPES), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), l-ascorbic acid 2-phosphate sesquimagnesium salt hydrate (AAP), (+)-5,6-O-isopropylidene-l-ascorbic acid (IAA), 2-O-α-d-glucopyranosyl-l-ascorbic acid (GPAA), 3-O-ethyl-l-ascorbic acid (EAA) were purchased from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and did not require further purification or treatment. The grapefruit juice, orange juice, and vitamin C tablets used in the experiment were purchased from local market.

The UV/Vis absorbance and fluorescence intensity were measured by a microplate spectrophotometer (Thermo Varioskan Flash, USA). The morphology and size of nanomaterials were observed using scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Hitachi, Japan). The elemental distribution was analyzed by energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) (Oxford Xplore, UK). The purity of the crystals was assessed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) (Shimadzu 6100, Japan). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Thermo Nicolet iS10, USA) was utilized to examine the infrared spectrum. The chemical states of the elements in Zr-CAU-28 were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (Quantera II SXM, PHI, USA). The zeta potential was measured with a zeta potential and nanoparticle size analyzer (Beckman Coulter, USA). The 1260 Infinity High-performance Liquid Chromatograph (Agilent Corporation, USA) was employed for quantitative analysis of AA.

The synthesis of Zr-CAU-28 was primarily based on a previous report (Dreischarf et al., 2017) with minor modifications. Typically, 0.45 g of H2FDC and 0.6 g of ZrOCl2·8H2O were introduced into a beaker. Subsequently, 7 mL of acetic acid and 8 mL of ultrapure water were added to dissolve the powders. The mixture was stirred for several minutes, then heated in a water bath at 80 °C with stirring for 1.5 h. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm to collect the precipitate. Finally, the precipitate was rinsed three times with anhydrous ethanol and dried to obtain the white powder Zr-CAU-28.

The phosphatase-like activity was first verified using 4-MUP as the substrate. Briefly, 20 μL of 3 mg·mL−1 Zr-CAU-28 and 470 μL of 100 mM HEPES buffer (pH 8) were added to a centrifuge tube. Then, 10 μL of 20 mM 4-MUP was added, and the reaction solution was mixed thoroughly. The emission spectra of the mixture were measured and the fluorescence intensity of the solution was observed. To investigate the catalytic ability of Zr-CAU-28 towards p-NPP, 70 μL of 3 mg·mL−1 Zr-CAU-28, 400 μL of 100 mM HEPES buffer (pH 8) and 30 μL of 20 mM p-NPP were mixed thoroughly. The absorption spectra of the mixture were scanned, and the color change was observed.

To clarify the source of phosphatase-like activity, Zr-CAU-28 powder was dissolved in ethanol to prepare a 3 mg·mL−1 solution. After standing for 24 h, the mixture was sonicated (360 W, 40 kHz) for 30 min. Then, 40 μL of the Zr-CAU-28 solution was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant and precipitate were collected in separate centrifuge tubes. Subsequently, HEPES buffer and 4-MUP were added and mixed thoroughly to measure the fluorescence emission spectra.

Under optimal catalytic conditions, the catalytic kinetics of Zr-CAU-28 with phosphatase-like activity were studied using 4-MUP as a substrate. By varying the amounts of Zr-CAU-28 and 4-MUP added, the fluorescence intensity of the solutions was recorded at 448 nm every 5 s under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. During the period, different concentrations of AA were added to the catalytic system to investigate its effect on catalytic kinetics. To determine the amount of 4-MU generated, a standard curve was first established between 4-MU concentration and fluorescence intensity (Fig. S1). Then, the quantity of 4-MU was back-calculated based on the monitored fluorescence value of the catalytic system. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the initial maximum reaction rate (Vmax) were calculated by the Michaelis-Menten eq. V = (Vmax × [S]) / (Km + [S]), where V is the initial reaction rate, and [S] is the substrate concentration.

In brief, 30 μL of 3 mg·mL−1 Zr-CAU-28 and 20 μL of AA solution with different concentrations were mixed thoroughly in individual centrifuge tubes, followed by reaction at 37 °C for 15 min. Subsequently, 440 μL of HEPES buffer and 10 μL of 20 mM 4-MUP were added to form a 500 μL system. The AA solution was substituted with an equal volume of ultrapure water to obtain the blank sample. The excitation wavelength was set at 360 nm, and the fluorescence value of the test sample (F) and the blank sample (F0) at 448 nm were measured. The change in fluorescence value (F0 F) was adopted as the fluorescence signal of the constructed sensor in response to AA in the system.

The orange juice, grapefruit juice, and vitamin C tablets were chosen to evaluate the practicability of the fluorescent sensor. According to the previous reports (Pei et al., 2024), the juice was centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min, followed by being filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. Subsequently, various amounts of AA were added to the filtrate, and the treated orange and grapefruit juices were diluted 100-fold and 10-fold respectively before analysis. The pretreatment of vitamin C tablets was referred to another report (G. Hu, Ge, et al., 2020). The vitamin C tablets were first ground into powder using a mortar. Then, 25 mg of vitamin C powder was added to 10 mL of ultrapure water, and the mixture was sonicated for 20 min. After the mixture was filtered three times, different amounts of AA were added to the filtrate. The treated solution was then diluted 100-fold for detection. Furthermore, a quantitative analysis of AA was conducted using HPLC (GB 5009.86–2016) to validate the accuracy of the established sensor.

The morphology of Zr-CAU-28 was observed by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1A, Zr-CAU-28 exhibits a hexagonal prism-like structure with a bottom side length of about 100 nm and a height of about 750 nm. EDS mapping demonstrated that the Zr, C, and O elements are uniformly distributed throughout Zr-CAU-28 (Fig. 1B), with respective weight percentages of 26.85 %, 52.27 %, and 20.76 %. The PXRD pattern of Zr-CAU-28 was basically consistent with previous report (Dreischarf et al., 2017) (Fig. 1C), indicating the successful preparation of highly crystalline Zr-CAU-28. In addition, the functional groups of Zr-CAU-28 were analyzed by FT-IR spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 1D, the characteristic peaks at 1710 and 1275 cm−1 in Zr-CAU-28 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CO and CO bonds in the ligand (Ye et al., 2023). The peak at 3425 cm−1 was attributed to the OH asymmetric stretching vibration, and the peak at 762 cm−1 was assigned to Zr-(μ3)O vibration (Wang et al., 2022; Yuan et al., 2023). In XPS analysis, the peaks at 533.37 eV, 286.06 eV, and 182.29 eV corresponded to the energy levels of O 1 s, C 1 s, and Zr 3d, respectively (Fig. 1E). The high-resolution Zr 3d spectra could be divided into four peaks (Fig. 1F), where those at 182.49 eV and 184.84 eV correspond to ZrO bonds, and those at 183.08 eV and 185.39 eV correspond to ZrZr bonds (Fang et al., 2020). Moreover, the synthesized Zr-CAU-28 had great dispersion in ethanol, and completely dissolved in the HEPES buffer system under the detection conditions (Fig. S2). UV–visible spectroscopy revealed an obvious absorption peak at 264 nm for Zr-CAU-28.

Phosphatases can catalyze the dephosphorylation of 4-MUP to produce 4-MU, which emits blue fluorescence under an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The phosphatase-like activity of Zr-CAU-28 was studied using 4-MUP as a substrate, and the results are shown in Fig. 2A. Neither Zr-CAU-28 (Sample 1) nor 4-MUP (Sample 2) solutions exhibited characteristic peaks within the wavelength range of 380–600 nm. In contrast, the catalytic system composed of Zr-CAU-28 and 4-MUP (Sample 3) displayed distinct blue fluorescence and a characteristic peak at 448 nm. Additionally, the catalytic activity of Zr-CAU-28 was verified using p-NPP as a substrate, which could be dephosphorylated to generate yellow product. Fig. S3 indicates that Zr-CAU-28 can catalyze the hydrolysis of p-NPP, resulting in a yellow-colored solution with a characteristic peak at 400 nm. These results confirm that Zr-CAU-28 possesses remarkable phosphatase-like activity, making it a promising candidate for phosphatase substitution.

Furthermore, the Zr-CAU-28 solution was centrifuged to identify the source of phosphatase activity (Fig. S4A). The results of Fig. S4B showed that the supernatant did not exhibit blue fluorescence after the addition of 4-MUP, whereas the redispersed precipitate was able to catalyze 4-MUP to generate fluorescent products with a characteristic peak at 448 nm. This phenomenon suggests that the hydrolysis of 4-MUP was catalyzed by Zr-CAU-28 rather than free Zr ions.

Herein, a novel fluorescent sensor for AA detection was constructed based on inhibiting the phosphatase-like activity of Zr-MOF. The working principle of the fluorescent sensor is illustrated in Fig. 2B. Zr-CAU-28 with phosphatase-like activity can catalyze the hydrolysis of 4-MUP to produce fluorescent 4-MU with an emission peak at 448 nm. Hydroxyl-rich AA molecules tend to form hydrogen bonds with [Zr6O4(OH)4] clusters, thereby adsorbing on the surface of Zr-CAU-28. The adsorption of AA reduces the relative content of terminal hydroxyl groups (terminal-OH) in Zr-CAU-28, which is considered to be the crucial structures for Zr-MOF to exhibit phosphate-like activity. Consequently, only a small amount of 4-MUP is hydrolyzed to 4-MU, resulting in a weaker fluorescence intensity of the system. A linear relationship exists between the variation in fluorescence intensity and the AA content, enabling AA detection in food samples.

To investigate the feasibility of the fluorescent sensor for AA detection, the fluorescence images and corresponding emission spectra of various solutions were recorded (Fig. 2C). Zr-CAU-28 effectively catalyzed the hydrolysis of 4-MUP to produce 4-MU, which exhibits a distinct blue fluorescence with a peak emission wavelength of 448 nm (Sample 1). The presence of AA led to a weakened fluorescence, and the fluorescence intensity of the solution at 448 nm gradually decreased with increasing AA content (Samples 2–4). Neither Zr-CAU-28, AA, nor 4-MUP solutions individually (Samples 5–7) exhibited emission peaks in the wavelength range of 380–600 nm, and the same result was observed for the mixture of AA and 4-MUP (Sample 8). Therefore, a new fluorescent sensor can be developed for AA detection based on the variation in fluorescence intensity at 448 nm of the catalytic system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibitory mechanism of AA on Zr-CAU-28

ce:section.ce:para: The internal filtering effect (IFE) is a common cause of fluorescence quenching. IFE occurs when the absorption spectrum of quencher overlaps with the excitation or emission spectra of the fluorescent substance (Zu et al., 2017). Subsequent experiments investigated the relationship between the decrease in fluorescence intensity and IFE. As shown in Fig. S5, the absorption spectra of AA and Zr-CAU-28 do not overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of the fluorescent product 4-MU. This result indicates that the reduction in fluorescence intensity is not caused by IFE. Furthermore, we altered the addition order of AA and recorded the subsequent changes in fluorescence intensity within the system. As depicted in Fig. S6, when Zr-CAU-28 and 4-MUP were first mixed and allowed to react for a period, the subsequent addition of AA had minimal impact on the fluorescence intensity. Surprisingly, if Zr-CAU-28 reacted with AA before the introduction of 4-MUP, the fluorescence intensity was significantly weakened. The above results reveal that the interaction between AA and Zr-CAU-28 reduces the fluorescence intensity of the system.

To reveal the interaction mechanism between AA and Zr-CAU-28, the effect of AA on the zeta potential of Zr-CAU-28 was investigated. In HEPES buffer at pH 8.5, the zeta potential of Zr-CAU-28 was −17.7 mV, and it changed to −29.1 mV after the addition of AA (Fig. 3A). It is known that the pKa1 and pKa2 of AA are 4.17 and 11.6, respectively (Spinola et al., 2014). At pH 8.5, AA molecules lose their first dissociable proton and carry a negative charge. The decrease in zeta potential may be attributed to the adsorption of negatively charged ascorbate ions on the surface of Zr-CAU-28, thereby increasing the negative charge density. Fig. 3B displays the FT-IR spectra of Zr-CAU-28 before and after AA addition. Notably, the OH vibrational peak shifted from 3426.4 cm−1 to 3428.8 cm−1 in the presence of AA, which may be attributed to the formation of hydrogen bonds between hydroxyl-rich AA molecules and [Zr6O4(OH)4] clusters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition of AA derivatives on Zr-CAU-28
ce:section.ce:para: To clarify the role of hydroxyl groups in inhibiting phosphatase-like activity, the effects of several AA derivatives on the catalytic reaction were studied. As shown in Fig. 3C, all the AA derivatives have caused varying degrees of inhibitory effect on the catalytic reaction. The phosphate-substituted AAP exhibited the strongest inhibitory effect, which may be due to its competition with 4-MUP for binding to the catalytic sites of Zr-CAU-28 (Fig. 3D). The other three derivatives were obtained by substituting different hydroxyl groups on the lactone ring of AA molecules. Therefore, it can be inferred that all hydroxyl groups on AA are involved in the inhibition of phosphatase-like activity. Interestingly, the inhibitory effects of GPAA (C2 substituted) and EAA (C3 substituted) were significantly weaker than those of other AA derivatives. It appears that that enol hydroxyl groups can interact more effectively with Zr-CAU-28, thereby inhibiting its phosphatase-like activity.

XPS analysis demonstrated a significant increase in the intensity of the O 1 s peak at 533 eV after the addition of AA (Fig. 3E). To determine the effect of AA on the chemical states of Zr-CAU-28, the O 1 s spectra were divided into three peaks, corresponding to terminal-OH (532.02 eV), bridging hydroxyl groups (μ3-OH, 533.74 eV), and Zr-O-Zr (μ3-O, 530.35 eV) (Y. X. Wu, Huang, et al., 2023) (Fig. S7A, B). After the addition of AA, the relative content of terminal-OH decreased from 44.43 % to 37.18 %, while that of μ3-OH increased from 51.6 % to 57.14 % (Fig. 3F). Generally, Zr-OH groups are considered crucial structures for the strong affinity of Zr-based MOFs towards phosphate groups. These hydroxyl groups comprise both μ3-OH and terminal-OH forms, with the latter exhibiting stronger reaction activity (X. Y. Zhu et al., 2015). Therefore, it is speculated that AA reduces the relative content of terminal-OH by forming hydrogen bonds with [Zr6O4(OH)4] clusters, leading to a decrease in the hydrolysis capability of the nanozyme towards the substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of AA on catalytic kinetics
ce:section.ce:para: To further explore the inhibitory mechanism of AA on phosphatase mimics, the catalytic rate of Zr-CAU-28 was measured at varying concentrations of AA. The relationship between catalytic rate and material concentration is shown in Fig. 4A. All three lines intersect at a single point, and their slopes gradually decrease with the increasing concentration of AA. This result indicates that AA exerts a typical reversible inhibitory effect on Zr-CAU-28. Furthermore, the Michaelis-Menten (Fig. 4B) and Lineweaver-Burk (Fig. 4C) curves for Zr-CAU-28 catalysis were plotted by varying the amounts of 4-MUP and AA. The corresponding Km and Vmax values calculated by the Michaelis Equation are shown in Fig. 4D. The results indicated that the Vmax for Zr-CAU-28 catalysis gradually decreased with increasing AA content, while the Km remained nearly unchanged, which conforms to the characteristics of non-competitive reversible inhibition.

Given that the catalytic activity of phosphatase mimic varies with pH, the influence of buffer pH on the constructed sensor was studied. As shown in Fig. S8, the fluorescence signal peaked at pH 8.5, possibly because Zr-CAU-28 exhibited highest catalytic activity at this pH. Additionally, the effect of buffer concentration on the fluorescent sensor was also examined. The sensing signal reached its maximum when the HEPES concentration was 40 mM, and further increases resulting in a decline in the sensing signal (Fig. S9). The possible reason is that the high levels of salt ions hinder the binding of Zr-CAU-28 to substrates.

Since Zr-CAU-28 was employed as a sensing material for AA detection, its concentration has a critical influence on the sensing signal. Fig. S10 showed that the optimal concentration of Zr-CAU-28 was 0.18 mg·mL−1. As the Zr-CAU-28 content increased, the signal value steadily declined, which may be related to the inability of AA to inhibit the catalytic activity of excessive Zr-CAU-28. In addition, the effect of 4-MUP quantity on the fluorescence signal was studied. The sensing system achieved the maximum signal at a 4-MUP concentration of 0.4 mM (Fig. S11). If the 4-MUP content continued to increase, the sensing signal slowly decreased. This may be attributed to the fact that excessive 4-MUP hindered the interaction between AA and Zr-CAU-28.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction temperature and time
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of phosphatase mimics is largely influenced by reaction temperature. Therefore, the sensing signals at different reaction temperatures were recorded and the optimal reaction temperature was determined to be 40 °C (Fig. S12). Considering that the catalytic process is closely related to the reaction time, the effect of reaction time on the signal value was investigated. As shown in Fig. S13, the fluorescence signal gradually increased with the prolongation of reaction time. For rapid detection of AA, the sensing signal was collected at the 30th minute.

The sensitivity of the established sensor was assessed by varying the amount of AA added. As the AA content increased from 0.4 to 15 μg·mL−1, the blue fluorescence of the sensing solutions gradually weakened, and the fluorescence intensity of corresponding emission peaks also decreased (Fig. 5A, B). The fluorescence signal tended to stabilize when the AA concentration exceeded 15 μg·mL−1, indicating that the inhibition of phosphatase-like activity by AA had reached saturation (Fig. 5C). Once the quantity of AA exceeded 5 μg·mL−1, the change in fluorescence intensity can be easily distinguished by the naked eyes. Notably, the fluorescent sensor showed excellent linearity within the range of 0.08–11 μg·mL−1 for AA detection, and the limit of detection (LOD) was as low as 0.025 μg·mL−1.

Table S1 summarizes the key performance parameters of the fluorescent sensor and several AA assay methods. Colorimetric methods are simple and visible, but their detection results are easily interfered by the sample background color. Fluorescence methods afford rapid and intuitive detection along with a relatively high sensitivity. The constructed sensor exhibited a wider linear range and lower LOD value than colorimetric methods. Despite the outstanding performance of electrochemical methods, especially their wide linear range, they often require complex electrode modifications and specialized equipment. In contrast, fluorescent methods allow analytes to be visually monitored through fluorescence intensity and brightness, and the entire detection process takes very little time (Tian et al., 2024). The proposed strategy employs a phosphatase mimic as the sensing material, avoiding the need for the unstable H2O2 required for most nanozyme-based sensors. Furthermore, the detection of AA is carried out based on the inhibition of nanozyme catalysis by the analyte. This response mechanism effectively avoids interference from other reducing substances, such as polyphenols, glutathione, and L-cysteine in food samples.

Various potential interferents, including amino acids, metal ions, glucose, and glutathione, were introduced into the reaction solution to assess the specificity of the fluorescent sensor. As depicted in Fig. 6A, the solution containing AA exhibited a faint fluorescence, whereas those with interfering substances emitted an intense fluorescence. Moreover, when AA and an interfering substance coexisted in the solution, the sensing signal was almost at the same level as the solution containing only AA (Fig. 6B). These results indicate that the proposed strategy is highly selective for AA and is capable of quantifying AA in complex food matrices.

To evaluate the feasibility of the fluorescent sensor in actual samples, the AA content in orange juice, grapefruit juice and vitamin C tablets was determined. For the original samples, the detection results of the fluorescent sensor were generally consistent with package descriptions (Table 1 ). The recovery rates in spiked sample analysis ranged from 96.25 % to 108.50 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) within the range of 1.53 %–3.04 %. Furthermore, HPLC was employed to further evaluate the performance of the developed sensor. The recovery rates of HPLC ranged from 97.5 % to 104 %, and its RSD was within the range of 1.3 %–2.74 %. Encouragingly, the recovery rates of the sensor are basically consistent with HPLC. Compared with the traditional methods, the fluorescent sensor has the advantages of simple operation, short time consumption, and on-site monitoring. Overall, the fluorescence sensor demonstrates good accuracy and practicability, possessing potential application value for the detection of AA in actual samples.

In summary, the Zr-CAU-28 was discovered to have phosphatase-like activity, which can catalyze the dephosphorylation of 4-MUP to generate a fluorescent product. Based on the inhibitory effect of AA on the phosphatase-like activity of Zr-CAU-28, a facile and sensitive fluorescent sensor for AA detection in food was developed. The LOD of the fluorescent sensor is 0.025 μg·mL−1, which is lower than that of most optical sensors. In actual samples analysis, the recovery rates ranged from 96.25 % to 108.50 %, while the RSD was within the range of 1.3 %–2.74 %. The detection performance of the fluorescent sensor was basically consistent with the HPLC, indicating a significant applicability of this method. Herein, the phosphatase-like activity of Zr-CAU-28 was studied for the first time and applied for food analysis. Unlike most nanozyme-based sensors, the proposed strategy does not depend on the reducibility of AA, thereby avoiding interference from widely distributed reducing substances in food. Given these advantages, we are confident that the proposed strategy provides a reliable option for AA detection.

Zhen Wu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jinlong Wang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xing Cheng: Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yue Tang: Supervision, Investigation, Formal analysis. Lian Xia: Validation, Formal analysis. Xiaojuan Niu: Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis. Yuangen Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (32360621, 32160603, 31760486), the Guizhou Provincial Basic Research Program (ZK[2021]Key Project 037), and the Guizhou Provincial Key Technology R&D Program (QKHZC[2023]123).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2025.142837.
